coredata.dc:title: Atomistic simulation for disordered rare-earth compounds SmCo7 and Sm(Co,Ti)7
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jianggui
Chen, Nanxian
Shen, Jiang
coredata.dc:description: The transition process from ordered SmCo5 (CaCu5-type) to disordered SmCo7 (TbCu7-type) is successfully simulated by using inverted ab initio pair potentials. The structure properties of this disordered TbCu7-type compound, including lattice constants, X-ray diffraction, phase stability, site preference have been calculated and the calculated results are in good agreement with the experimental data. Also, a new structure deformation of 3g sublattice in SmCo7−x Ti x is reported.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rare-earth compounds
Transition metal compounds
Crystal structure
Computer simulations
Preferential site ordering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Brief introduction of the inverted potentials
Evolution from SmCo5 to SmCo7 and some properties of SmCo7 and Sm(Co,Ti)7
Brief description of the evolution from SmCo5 to ordered Sm2Co17 structure
The site preference for the ternary element Ti

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evolution from SmCo5 to ordered structure Sm2Co17.

(a) The unit cell of SmCo5. (b) (SmCo5)6×2×6 supercell (projected along b-axis). (c) An intermediate state of relaxation. (d) The final structure after relaxation. (e) The calculated and experimental XRD patterns.
Perspective configurations of the transition about SmCo7 from 0.6Å random disordered state (a) to relaxed state (e), and the corresponding XRD patterns have also been shown.
The tolerance for SmCo7−x Ti x after relaxation.
The relaxed SmCo6.6Ti0.4. The calculated and experimental XRD patterns of SmCo6.6Ti0.4.

(a1), (b1), and (c1) are the structures of SmCo5. (a2), (b2), and (c2) are the corresponding deformed structures of SmCo7. (a3), (b3), and (c3) are the corresponding deformed structures of SmCo6.6Ti0.4. ‘o’ in (c2) and (c3) represents the dumbbell atoms nearby.
The parameters of the inverted potentials used in this work
Lattice constants of SmCo5, SmCo7 and SmCo6.6Ti0.4 system

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The relative deviations between the calculation and the experimental results are less than 1.9%.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The average atom sites for the calculated (SmCo7)56 structure compared with the symmetry of P6/mmm space group
Determination of lattice constants of SmCo7 after global deformation
The atomic site parameters of the relaxed (SmCo7)56 supercell

article.ce:title: Atomistic simulation for disordered rare-earth compounds SmCo7 and Sm(Co,Ti)7

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Applied Physics, Beijing University of Science and Technology, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The transition process from ordered SmCo5 (CaCu5-type) to disordered SmCo7 (TbCu7-type) is successfully simulated by using inverted ab initio pair potentials. The structure properties of this disordered TbCu7-type compound, including lattice constants, X-ray diffraction, phase stability, site preference have been calculated and the calculated results are in good agreement with the experimental data. Also, a new structure deformation of 3g sublattice in SmCo7−x Ti x is reported.

ce:section.ce:para: In the Sm–Co system, there are two kinds of well known high temperature permanent magnetic materials. SmCo5 has very high magneto-crystalline anisotropy (MA) (H A >200kOe), but relatively low magnetic moment (7.3μB/f.u.), while Sm2Co17 compound with low MA (about 65kOe) has high magnetic moment (23.2μB/f.u.). Since the composition of SmCo7 lies between that of SmCo5 and Sm2Co17, and this kind of compounds may retain reasonably high moment and anisotropy, recently TbCu7-type rare-earth (R) transition metal (T) intermetallics RT7 have attracted great attention [1–4].

As well as the ordered compounds R2T17 (Th2Zn17 or Th2Ni17-type), R3T29 (Nd3Fe29−x Ti x -type), and RT12 (ThMn12-type) [5], the disordered compound RT7 (TbCu7-type) [6] is regarded to be derived from RT5 (CaCu5-type) structure through the substitution of T dumbbells for R atoms. The fractions of dumbbell substitution are 1/3, 2/5, 1/2, and 2/9, respectively. Here R2T17, R3T29, and RT12 are connected with the ordered substitution and RT7 is connected with disordered substitution. The above series of rare-earth transition metal intermetallics have been attracted much attention due to their great importance in magnetic material application. However the theoretical calculations for these rare-earth intermetallic compounds are very difficult to be carried out because of the complexity of their structures.

In 1990, Chen proposed a concise inversion formula through analogizing the Möbius inversion theorem in number theory [7,8]. In the following years, Chen et al. set up a set of inverted ab initio pair potentials by introducing some virtual models [9,10]. These potentials have been successfully used to simulate the evolution from RT5 to ordered compounds R2T17, R3T29, and RT12. In this work, the same set of potentials is used to simulate the evolution from RT5 to disordered compound RT7 and also calculate the properties of the ternary structure for this disordered compound. The calculation for the disordered compound is much more difficult than for the ordered ones due to their stochastic distribution of the atoms.

The second part is a brief introduction of the inverted potentials. In the third part we demonstrate the evolution simulation from SmCo5 to SmCo7 compound using the inverted potentials and, for the disordered structure, we calculate the lattice constants, X-ray diffraction (XRD), phase stability, site preference of ternary element, and so on. The conclusions and discussion will be given in the last part.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Brief introduction of the inverted potentials
ce:section.ce:para:
Suppose that the crystal cohesive energy can be expressed as the sum of interatomic pair potentials:
(1)
E ( x ) =
1 2
∑
r i
≠ 0
Φ (
r
i
) =
1 2
∑
n = 1
∞
r 0
( n ) Φ (
b 0
( n ) x )
,
where x is the nearest-neighbor distance, r
i the position vector of the ith atom, b 0(n)x the nth-neighbor distance, and r 0(n) is the nth coordination number. We extend the series, {b 0(n)}, into a multiplicative semigroup {b(n)} such that, for any two integers m and n, there always exists an integer k such that
(2)
b ( k ) = b ( m ) b ( n ) .
Eq. (1) can then be rewritten as
(3)
E ( x ) =
1 2
∑
n = 1
∞
r ( n ) Φ ( b ( n ) x )
,
where
(4)
r ( n ) =
r 0
(
b 0
− 1
[ b ( n ) ] ) ,
if
b ( n ) ∈ {
b 0
( n ) }
0 ,
if
b ( n ) ∉ {
b 0
( n ) } .
Thus the pair potential, Φ(x), can be written as
(5)
Φ ( x ) = 2
∑
n = 1
∞
I ( n ) E ( b ( n ) x )
where I(n) is determined by
(6)
∑
b ( n ) | b ( k )
I ( n ) r
b
− 1
b ( k )
b ( n )
=
δ
k l
.

I(n) is related only to crystal geometrical structure, not to concrete element category, i.e. I(n) is uniquely determined by {r 0(n)} and {b 0(n)}. Thus the inverted potentials can be obtained from the known cohesive energy function.

In principle, all the potentials can be obtained from cohesive energy curves. As far as rare-earth intermetallic compounds are concerned in our work, the cohesive energy has been acquired either by ab initio calculation or by experimental data. But all the potentials are extracted by using lattice inversion method.

In this work, we consider the virtual bcc Co as B2 structure with two simple cubic sublattices Co1 and Co2. Thus we calculate the partially cohesive energy as
(7)
E ( x ) =
E
Co
bcc
( x ) −
E
C
o 1
sc
( x ) −
E
C
o 2
sc
( x ) =
∑
i , j , k ≠ 0
∞
Φ
Co − Co
4 3
i −
1 2
2
+
j −
1 2
2
+
k −
1 2
2
x
where x is the nearest neighbor distance in the bcc structure,
E
Co
bcc
( x )
represents the total energy of Co with bcc structure,
E
C
o 1
sc
( x )
or
E
C
o 2
sc
( x )
is the total energy of Co with simple cubic structure. The total energies are calculated based on the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) density functional theory using ASW method. Now becomes the cohesive energy function of one Co1 atom with all the Co2 atoms. Here, the Co2 atoms form a simple cubic structure, and only one Co1 atom is located at the center of each cube.

For simplicity, the equation with Rose form
(8)
E ( x ) =
E 0
[ 1 + β ( x −
x 0
) ]
e
− β ( x −
x 0
)
is used to fit the cohesive energy curve E(x), where E 0 is the sublimation energy, and x 0 is the equilibrium nearest-neighbor distance, with
(9)
β =
9 B
Ω 0
x 0 2
E 0
1 / 2
,
where B is the bulk modulus and the equilibrium volume. Thereby, these parameters E 0, B, and Ω 0 can be obtained by fitting the calculated cohesive energy curve according to above equation.
Then Φ Co–Co(x) can be given directly by Eq. (5).

The ab initio calculation of total energy curve related to Φ R–Co(x) is hard to perform. The lattice constant, module constant and total energy of R3T with L12 structure near equilibrium position has been calculated. Then the partial cohesive energy of R–T can be obtained by subtracting the contribution of R–R and T–T in the structure respectively. Subsequently, the Φ R–Co(x) can be deduced from lattice inversion method.

Alternatively, the cohesive energy curves can be directly determined if the experimental data E 0, B, and Ω 0 are available in literature. In this work, the cohesive energy curves between identical rare-earth atoms are obtained from the experimental data. Thus the Φ R–R(x) can also be given by Eq. (5).

The obtained pair potential can be described by Morse function:
(10)
Φ ( x ) =
D 0
[
e
− y ( x /
R 0
− 1 )
− 2
e
− y / 2 ( x /
R 0
− 1 )
]
where x is the distance between two atoms, D 0, γ, and R 0 are the parameters. The calculated potential parameters used in this work are given in Table 1 . We should mention that, in the potential function, there have no adjustable parameters for different structures and this may help to be used to predict new structures or new properties of the material.

But the ab initio calculation in the present work is based on simple structures and virtual structures, thus the transferability of the potentials for various structures is indeed questionable. However our previous calculation based on the transferability of the potentials is very successful in abundant aspects, particularly in the rare-earth transition metal intermetallics [11–15]. Calculation results of some properties such as the lattice constants, phase stability, site preference and variation behavior of Curie temperature are well consistent with experimental results, which show the potentials are effective to rare-earth transition metal intermetallics with various structures. It should be mentioned that the same potentials Φ Sm–Sm, Φ Sm–Co, and Φ Co–Co used in this disordered SmCo7 system have also been applied successfully to the simulation of the ordered structure R(Co,T)12 [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evolution from SmCo5 to SmCo7 and some properties of SmCo7 and Sm(Co,Ti)7
Brief description of the evolution from SmCo5 to ordered Sm2Co17 structure
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explain the evolution process from ordered structure SmCo5 to disordered structure SmCo7, we first introduce the relatively simple evolution process from SmCo5 structure to ordered Sm2Co17 structure. Fig. 1(a) is the CaCu5-type SmCo5 structure projected along the c-axis [17]. Then a supercell (SmCo5)6 (Fig. 1(b)) is built as the initial model for simulating the process. We select special ordered series of rare-earth atoms (1/3) to be replaced by Co dumbbells as shown in Fig. 1. In the first layer, the dumbbell substitutes for Sm atom in the up-left center; in the second layer, the dumbbell substitutes for Sm atom in the down-right center. Now a so-called supercell (Sm2Co17)2 is obtained, while the length and orientations of these dumbbells and the positions of all atoms are meaningless since this structure is not stable. For obtaining the stable structure, the inverted ab initio potentials are applied. We use the energy minimization to express the molecular static process (Fig. 1(c)), and finally the Th2Ni17-type [18] Sm2Co17 structure (Fig. 1(d)) is formed which is as same as the experimental result.

In principle, in order to describe the stochastic property of the substitution in SmCo7, the initial SmCo5 supercell should be very large. But for concrete calculation and analysis, one would like to take a super-cell as small as possible. After many testing, we find that a supercell (SmCo5)72 is available for the simulation. In this work, the (SmCo5)72 is built by 6×2×6 SmCo5 unit cell [17] shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b). In order to make the picture concise, for the supercell, the 2c atoms at the basal plane and the 3g atoms at the intermediate plane are hidden temporary.

Now let us start the simulation of the evolution. First, 2/9 of Sm atoms (totally 72×2/9=16 atoms) in (SmCo5)6×2×6 are selected randomly and each one is replaced by a Co dumbbell with arbitrary length (0.1–3.0Å) and orientation. Note that for the disordered substitution, there are many possible sets of Sm atom distributions for the 2/9 substitution. Here, only one distribution is presented as an example. By this way, a (SmCo7)56 is obtained, which is an un-relaxed model.

Then it is relaxed under the control of the inverted potentials. Once the potentials are taken into account, the orientation and the length of the dumbbells change immediately, and the site distribution of all the atoms is rearranged at the same time. Meanwhile, the lattice constants are adjusted as shown in Fig. 2(c). Finally, a relaxed (SmCo7)56 is obtained in which the orientations of all the dumbbells are almost arranged along the c-axis with the length nearby 2.35Å, as shown in Fig. 2(d). The atomic site parameters for this relaxed supercell are listed in Appendix 1.

The lattice constants of SmCo7 calculated from this relaxed (SmCo7)56 and obtained from the experimental results [19] are listed in Table 2 . The comparison shows that the calculated results are well consistent with the experimental ones.

Fig. 2(e1) is the calculated powder XRD pattern of this relaxed (SmCo7)56 (Fig. 2(d), Appendix 1) by using the Accelrys’ Cerius2 software [20]. In the calculation, the atomic scattering factors are taken from Ibers and Hamilton [21]. The radiation wavelength is 1.54178Å (Cu Kα). For the powder XRD, extinction correction and temperature factor have not been applied.

Fig. 2(e2) shows the experimental XRD pattern of SmCo7 [1]. It should be noting that, in this work, the corresponding experimental wavelengths are also 1.54178Å. Fig. 2(e1) and (e2) indicate a good agreement between the calculated and experimental results.

Fig. 2(e3) shows the experimental XRD pattern of SmCo5 [22]. Comparing it with that of SmCo7 (Fig. 2(e1) and (e2)), it can be found that, the distance between (200) and (002) peaks in SmCo5 is significantly larger. Both experiment [23] and our calculation (Table 2) show that, comparing with SmCo5 structure, the lattice constant a in SmCo7 is reduced and c is increased, which resulting a shifting of the (200) peak to high angle and the (002) peak to low angle.

The average atom sites of the relaxed (SmCo7)56 are listed in Table 3 . From the table, it can be seen that the symmetry of this structure is close to P6/mmm space group. In fact, by a complex calculation, the space group of this structure is definitely determined to be P6/mmm within a range of tolerance (Appendix 2). This is consistent with the experimental result for the TbCu7-type SmCo7 structure [1].
From all above analysis, the disordered SmCo7 model structure can be obtained from SmCo5 by our inverted ab initio potentials.

In order to check the stability of SmCo7 structure, we make all the atoms in the relaxed (SmCo7)56 shift randomly by 0.6Å, as shown in (Fig. 3(a)). Now the structure shows disordering significantly which may be considered as the molten SmCo7 like state. By using the inverted potentials, the energy minimization algorithm recovers the initial relaxed (SmCo7)56 (Fig. 3(b)).

Table 4 demonstrates the structure stability under variety of global deformations, which represent stretching, compressing, shearing, and the mixture of these ways. After relaxation, again, all the structural parameters are restored.
The above simulations show that the SmCo7 with TbCu7-type structure is stable in a certain range of phase space.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The site preference for the ternary element Ti
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the site preference of ternary element Ti in the calculated (SmCo7)56, Ti is used to substitute for Co at 2e, 2c, and 3g sites, respectively. After energy minimization, we compare the tolerance in these three cases to see which one corresponds to the smallest tolerance. The tolerance indicates the atomic derivation distance which can be viewed as the error in the process of determining the space group of the compound (Appendix 2). As shown in Fig. 4 , the calculated results indicate that Ti prefers to substitute Co at 2e site and refuse 2c and 3g sites. This is in agreement with experiment [24].

Because the number of the 2e atoms is relatively small, we can conclude that the solubility of Ti in SmCo7−x Ti x compound should be low (limited to x =0.57). This reflects the Ti composition x less than 0.6 in experiment [24].

As an example, the calculated structure, XRD pattern, and lattice constants for SmCo6.6Ti0.4 with Ti atoms at the 2e site are shown in Fig. 5 and Table 2. Comparing with the experiment [24], it is found that our calculated results about the lattice constants are slightly bigger. But the relative deviation falls within the range of 1.9%.
In fact, the calculated tolerances listed in Fig. 4 are the statistical average of 10 stochastic examples. The symbol ‘I’ denotes the range of error bar.
According to our calculation with the above relaxed (SmCo7)56 supercell, there exist significant deformations for 2c and 3g sublattices that can be described as follows.

The 2c shrink deformation: The Co atoms at the 2c site shrink to the center of dumbbell along the basal plane. Fig. 6(a1) shows 2c and 1a sites in SmCo5 structure, while Fig. 6(a2) shows the case in the SmCo7 structure. From these pictures, the high symmetry of the 2c site (
6 ¯
m 2
) is destroyed greatly when the structure evolves from SmCo5 to SmCo7.
The 3g displacement deformation: The substitution of the dumbbell makes the nearby 3g Co atoms deviate slightly from the intermediate plane along the c-axis, as shown in Fig. 6(b1) and (b2).

The 3g twist deformation: as shown in Fig. 6(c2), near the dumbbells, there exists the twisted 3g sublattice in the intermediate plane when substitute Co dumbbells for some of the Sm atoms. From the picture, the symmetry of 3g sublattice is also destroyed due to this deformation.

The 2c shrink deformation and the 3g displacement deformation of SmCo7 are in good agreement with those determined by using both unpolarized and polarized neutrons [25]. As far as we know, the 3g twist deformation has not been reported by the literature. The reason perhaps is this kind of disordered sublattice deformation cannot be revealed easily by the experiment. Here, as a prediction, it is presented by our theoretical calculation. The correctness about this deformation will undergo further examination in the future.

When the ternary element Ti is introduced into the SmCo7 structure and the structure is relaxed again under the inverted potentials, the above deformations will change with different extent. For example, in SmCo6.6Ti0.4, most of the 3g twist deformations will be restored to high symmetry state (Fig. 6(c3)), while the 2c shrink deformation and 3g displacement deformation do not have significant change as shown in Fig. 6(a3) and (b3).

In this work, the transition process from SmCo5 to SmCo7 is simulated by using the inverted ab initio pair potentials. The lattice constants of the relaxed (SmCo7)56 are calculated. The XRD pattern for this structure is shown. The average atom sites of (SmCo7)56 show that the relaxed structure satisfies with the symmetry of space group P6/mmm. The phase stability shows that the SmCo7 structure is stable in a certain range of phase space. The site preference of Ti for this system is 2e. All the above calculated results are well consistent with the experimental results. The 2c shrink deformation and 3g displacement deformation coincide well with the unpolarized and polarized neutrons determination. The 3g twist deformation has no corresponding experimental result, so it can be regarded as a theoretical prediction. It can be concluded that the inverted potentials are applicable for the complicated system with rare-earth and transition metal atoms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their thanks to Prof. Rao and Prof. Liang for their helpful discussions. This work was supported in part by National Nature Science Foundation of China Grant No. 10274035, and in part by the 973 Project in China under Grant Nos. TG2000067101 and TG2000067106.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The atomic site parameters of the relaxed (SmCo7)56 supercell are shown in Table A1 .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, the way to determine the space group of SmCo7 is based on X-ray diffraction comparing with the standard spectra of SmCo5 [6]. In our calculation, we determine the space group of SmCo7 in a similar way. The concrete procedure is to transfer the calculated SmCo7 model to a “reasonably equivalent” SmCo5 model, then compare it with standard SmCo5 model to obtain the tolerance. If the tolerance is large, we say the space group of SmCo5 is not suitable for SmCo7; other wise, we say the space group of SmCo5 is suitable for SmCo7. The detail can be described as follows.

First we have to distinguish the Co dumbbells, and find the center coordinates for each dumbbell. On each center we put a Sm atom in and reduce all the dumbbells. In this step, we have 72 Sm atoms and 360 Co atoms in our simulation model. Secondly, we consider the model includes 72 SmCo5 cells. Then we do statistics for each site in an average SmCo5 structure to obtain the average atomic coordinates for each site in this equivalent model, at the same time we estimate (1) the error or deviation for calculating each average atomic site, (2) the error or deviation relative to standard SmCo5 structure. Finally we obtain the tolerance of our “equivalent model for SmCo7” relative to space group of standard SmCo5 structure by estimating the [(error−1)2 +(error−2)2]1/2. In this calculation, both the deviations of Sm atoms and Co atoms have been expressed in this procedure.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pearson's Handbook of Crystallographic Data for Intermetallic Phases

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Cerius2 User Guide, Molecular Simulations, Inc. San Diego, CA, 1997.
